description: Phrase ablation matrix for isolating which parts of the bad-converse trace create a yes-answer attractor in simple syllogisms.
question_template: All {s_plural} are {m_plural}. No {m_plural} are {o_plural}. Can any {s_singular} be {o_article} {o_singular}? Reply with exactly one line: Answer: <yes/no>.

variants.description: Original premises plus the valid negative conclusion.
Adds the invalid converse step but does not add a permissive conclusion.
steps: If all {s_plural} are {m_plural}, then being {m_article} {m_singular} implies being {s_article} {s_singular}.

variants.description: Tests whether the permissive 'may still' conclusion alone is enough to create the yes attractor.
Tests softer possibility wording without the explicit 'may still be' answer form.
steps: This leaves open whether {s_article} {s_singular} could be {o_article} {o_singular}.

variants.description: Original invalid converse plus permissive wording, but without the discourse marker 'Therefore'.
steps: If all {s_plural} are {m_plural}, then being {m_article} {m_singular} implies being {s_article} {s_singular}.

variants.description: Original bad-converse trace from the interference matrix.
steps: If all {s_plural} are {m_plural}, then being {m_article} {m_singular} implies being {s_article} {s_singular}.

variants.description: Adds the invalid converse but repairs the final answer with the valid negative conclusion.
steps: If all {s_plural} are {m_plural}, then being {m_article} {m_singular} implies being {s_article} {s_singular}.
But every {s_singular} is {m_article} {m_singular}, and no {m_singular} is {o_article} {o_singular}.
